name: Mani
description: Mani, in Norse mythology, is the personification of the Moon. He is the son of Mundilfari and brother of Sól, the Sun. Mani is described as being incredibly handsome and captivating. According to the Prose Edda, the children are so beautiful that Mundilfari names them Moon (Mani) and Sun (Sól), which angers the gods. As punishment for Mundilfari's arrogance, the gods place Sól and Mani in the sky to drive the Sun and Moon chariots, respectively. Mani's chariot is pursued by the wolf Hati Hróðvitnisson, who seeks to devour him. The phases of the moon are explained as the result of Hati getting closer or further away from Mani. Mani is also said to have taken two children, Bil and Hjúki, from Earth as they were drawing water from a well. They now accompany him on his journey across the sky, serving as his attendants. Mani's role is vital in the Norse cosmos, maintaining the celestial balance and the passage of time. His eventual fate, like that of Sól, is to be consumed by the wolves at Ragnarök, the end of the world. He represents the lunar cycle, the ebb and flow, and the beauty of the night sky. He possesses the power to control the tides and influence the growth of plants, as well as being associated with the passage of time and the prediction of the future based on lunar cycles. Mani's influence is subtle but pervasive, a constant presence in the night sky guiding travelers and shaping the world.
subtitle: The Norse God of the Moon

keyMyths.title: The Naming of Mani and Sól
keyMyths.description: Mundilfari named his children Moon and Sun, angering the gods who forced them to drive the celestial chariots as punishment. This myth explains the origin and roles of Mani and Sól in the Norse cosmos.

keyMyths.title: Mani and the Children Bil and Hjúki
keyMyths.description: Mani abducted the children Bil and Hjúki as they were drawing water, taking them to the sky to serve as his attendants. This myth explains the presence of these two figures alongside Mani in the night sky.

keyMyths.title: The Pursuit of Mani by Hati
keyMyths.description: The wolf Hati Hróðvitnisson relentlessly pursues Mani across the sky, seeking to devour him. The phases of the moon are explained by Hati's varying proximity to Mani during his celestial journey.

sources.author: Snorri Sturluson
sources.description: A key source of Norse mythology, providing detailed accounts of Mani and his role in the cosmos.
sources.text: A key source of Norse mythology, providing detailed accounts of Mani and his role in the cosmos.

sources.author: Various
sources.description: A collection of Old Norse poems, including verses about Mani and his pursuit by Hati.
